The surname LOOKS French (from 'Richelette' in French-speaking Belgium) but he lived in German lands, so two camps exist: the German-style deer-ick-LAY (hard 'k', /kleɪ/) vs the French-style deer-ish-LAY (soft 'sh', /ʃleɪ/). Final stress on -LAY; the 't' is silent in both common readings (though a minority, citing Calabi, insist on a hard final 't'). Wikipedia's own talk page never resolved it — a contributor jokes that saying it 'correctly' makes you sound wrong to most mathematicians. Wikipedia documents the English IPA as the hard-k /ˌdɪərɪˈkleɪ/; many ML practitioners say deer-ish-LAY.

galois.notes: Named after French mathematician Évariste Galois (1811–1832), of Galois theory and the finite/Galois field GF(p) that underpins error-correcting codes, AES, and elliptic-curve crypto. English speakers regularly say gal-OYZ or GAL-oyss, treating it like an English word; the accepted reading is gal-WAH — the final 's' is SILENT (French) and stress lands on the second syllable -WAH. Wiktionary gives English /ɡælwɑ/; French is [ɡalwa]. Crypto/TLS engineers who say 'Galois Counter Mode (GCM)' very often mispronounce it.

levenshtein.notes: Named after Soviet/Russian mathematician Vladimir Levenshtein (Russian: Левенштейн), who defined the edit-distance metric in 1965 — bread-and-butter in NLP, spell-checkers, fuzzy search, and bioinformatics. The German/Yiddish-orthography '-stein' is the trap: the standard English reading rhymes 'stein' with 'mine' (like Einstein), giving LEV-en-shtine — stress on the FIRST syllable, 'sht' cluster kept intact. Many devs instead say LEV-en-STEEN / LEE-ven-steen (sounding the 's' separately, '-steen' like the recurring 'Bernstein' debate) or stress the middle syllable. The 'sh' before the 't' is what most often gets dropped.

kurtosis.notes: Statistics term for the 'tailedness' of a distribution, from Greek κυρτός (kurtos, 'curved/arching'); coined by Karl Pearson c.1905. Wiktionary GenAm /kɚˈtoʊsɪs/ — primary stress on the second/middle syllable -TOH-, with a long 'oh'. Data scientists frequently flatten it to KUR-toh-sis (stress on first) or say ker-TAH-sis / ker-TOSS-is with a short 'o'. The leading 'ku-' is a plain 'ker-' schwa+r, not 'koor-'. Pairs with 'skewness' in every pandas .describe() conversation, so it's said often and missed often.

weibull.notes: The Weibull distribution (reliability engineering, survival analysis, hazard/lifetime modeling, ML) is named after Swedish engineer Waloddi Weibull (1887–1979). General American speakers overwhelmingly say WHY-bull (/ˈwaɪbʊl/) with a true 'w' — this is the headword pronunciation in English Wikipedia, stress on the first syllable. But because Swedish 'W' is sounded as a 'V', the namesake-correct form is VY-bull (/ˈveɪbʊl/), which purists and many reliability engineers prefer; both are commonly heard in stats/ML talks. Either way the '-bull' is a reduced 'buul' (/-bʊl/), not 'bool'.

tikhonov.notes: Tikhonov regularization (= ridge regression / L2 penalty; also ill-posed inverse problems) is named after Russian mathematician Andrey Tikhonov. Russian Ти́хонов = [ˈtʲixənəf] (per Wiktionary): stress on the FIRST syllable — TEE-khuh-nawf (the reduced final vowel is closer to 'nuf') — with a guttural 'kh' (/x/, a single throaty sound, not 'k-h') and a final 'v' that devoices toward 'f'. English speakers almost always shift stress to the second syllable, tik-HOH-nov, and say a plain 'k' + 'h'. The name also surfaces transliterated as 'Tychonoff' (Tychonoff's theorem in topology), reinforcing that the 'kh' is one guttural sound.

mythos.notes: GenAm "MITH-ahs" (Merriam-Webster primary). Greek-derived term for a culture's underlying narrative/belief system. One reading pinned per request.

i-jepa.notes: Meta's image self-supervised model (Image JEPA); the 'I' is the letter 'eye' /aɪ/ and JEPA is one word /ˈdʒɛpə/ rhyming with 'pepper', so 'eye-JEP-uh' — the way LeCun voices the suffix. Predicts in latent space, not pixels.

v-jepa.notes: Meta's video JEPA; 'V' (Video) is spelled 'vee' and JEPA is said as the word, giving 'vee-JEP-uh'. Not letter-by-letter.

v-jepa 2.notes: 2025 video world model, successor to V-JEPA; numeral read 'two' — 'vee-JEP-uh two'.

a-jepa.notes: Audio/spectrogram JEPA built on I-JEPA; the 'A' (Audio) is the letter-name 'ay' /eɪ/, NOT the article 'uh' — 'ay-JEP-uh'.

mc-jepa.notes: Motion-and-Content JEPA; the initials have no vowel so they are letter-spelled 'em-see' ahead of the word JEPA — 'em-see-JEP-uh'.

point-jepa.notes: JEPA for point clouds; ordinary word 'point' + JEPA — 'point-JEP-uh'.

dinov2.notes: Meta/FAIR self-supervised ViT foundation model, DINO scaled to v2; said 'DEE-noh vee-two' (DINO = self-DIstillation with NO labels). A minority says 'DYE-noh'.

dinov3.notes: Meta's 2025 ~7B-param self-supervised vision backbone, third in the DINO line; 'DEE-noh vee-three' is most common, but the dinosaur-pun reading 'DYE-noh' is genuinely widespread, so the vowel is unsettled.

ibot.notes: ByteDance image-BERT pretraining with an online tokenizer (i = image, lowercase like iPhone); the repo's robot mascot signals 'EYE-bot'.

beit.notes: Microsoft masked-image-modeling pretraining (BERT for image Transformers, i = image); no official reading — split between 'beet' (most common, one syllable) and 'bait'.

dino-wm.notes: NYU/Meta-FAIR latent world model built on frozen DINOv2 patch features for zero-shot planning; said 'DEE-noh W-M', often expanded to 'dino world model'.

byol.notes: Negative-pair-free SSL that bootstraps a target network from an online network (DeepMind 2020); the authors fixed no pronunciation, so it is both spelled out 'B-Y-O-L' and said as a word 'bee-OHL' — the follow-up BYOL-A audio paper even puns 'pronounced viola'.

simsiam.notes: Negative-free Siamese SSL that avoids collapse via a stop-gradient; portmanteau of 'Simple Siamese', so the tail is read like 'Siam' — 'sim-SY-am'.

vicreg.notes: SSL that prevents collapse with separate Variance-Invariance-Covariance Regularization terms; said as the word 'VIK-reg' (hard g from 'regularization'), not spelled out.

vicregl.notes: VICReg extended to learn local/dense visual features for detection and segmentation; 'VIK-reg' followed by a spelled-out 'L' (for Local).

barlow twins.notes: SSL whose loss drives the cross-correlation matrix of two identical network 'twins' toward the identity; plain English, named for neuroscientist Horace Barlow's redundancy-reduction principle — 'BAR-loh twinz'.

moco.notes: FAIR contrastive SSL with a momentum-updated encoder and a queue of negatives; a blend of MOmentum + COntrast, said as a word — 'MOH-koh'.

simclr.notes: Google/Hinton contrastive SSL framework; 'Sim' (Simple) + 'CLR' (Contrastive Learning of Representations) puns on 'clear', so 'sim-CLEAR' dominates; a minority spells it 'sim-C-L-R'.

mae.notes: FAIR masked-image SSL (Masked AutoEncoder) that reconstructs pixels — the model JEPA argues against. Genuinely unsettled: many say the word 'may', others spell 'em-ay-EE'; no author statement, and it collides with 'mean absolute error'.

data2vec.notes: Meta AI cross-modal SSL that predicts its own latent representations (closest non-JEPA cousin in spirit); inherits the word2vec/wav2vec lineage, so '2' is 'to' and 'vec' is 'vek' — 'DAY-tuh-too-vek'.

iris.notes: Sample-efficient Atari agent that learns inside a discrete-autoencoder + GPT-like Transformer world model; styled as the real word 'iris' (the official repo is lowercase) — 'EYE-ris', not lettered out.

twm.notes: A Transformer-XL-based world model for data-efficient Atari RL; the initials have no vowel to form a word, so they are spelled out letter by letter — 'T-W-M'.

genie 2.notes: DeepMind's large-scale foundation world model that generates action-controllable, playable 3D environments from a single image; 'Genie' (from 'Generative Interactive Environments') is said like the common word genie, '2' as 'two' — 'JEE-nee two'.

aleph.notes: Aleph is Runway's July-2025 in-context video model, named after aleph (א), the first letter of the Hebrew/Semitic alphabet. Stress falls on the first syllable: AH-lef, with a broad "ah" /ɑː/; the -lif variant (AH-lif) is equally attested. Non-native devs often mis-stress it as "uh-LEFF" (second-syllable stress with a reduced first vowel) the second-syllable stress is wrong, while a long-a "AY-lef" /ˈeɪlɛf/ is a valid but less common dictionary variant. Non-obvious because the spelled "a" is the open /ɑ/, not a schwa or the English long-a, and Runway's own announcement never states a pronunciation or name origin.

aphrodite.notes: Aphrodite (aphrodite-engine) is PygmalionAI's large-scale LLM inference engine, built on vLLM's PagedAttention and named after the Greek goddess of love. Four syllables with primary stress on the third: af-ruh-DY-tee, ending in a full "-ee" sound. Non-native devs mangle it to "AY-pro-dite" — but "aph" is "af" (not "ayf"), and the final -e is NOT silent (it's "ee"). It's non-obvious because the "-ite" ending looks like it should rhyme with "kite," and English speakers instinctively silence a trailing -e. Verified against Wiktionary US IPA /æfɹəˈdaɪti/.

baml.notes: BAML is Boundary's (YC) open-source domain-specific language for writing type-safe LLM/agent prompts; .baml files declare typed prompt functions callable from any language. The README's own gloss is the joke "Basically A Made-up Language"; it is also widely reported to have originated as "Boundary AI Markup Language," paralleling YAML. Built on the -ML markup-language lineage, so the natural one-word reading is "bammel" /ˈbæməl/ — first-syllable stress, rhyming with camel, exactly like YAML→"yammel" / SAML→"sammel." No official pronunciation guide exists and many first-time readers instead spell it out B-A-M-L (alt), so it is genuinely split. Non-obvious because the four consonant-heavy letters give no cue that a vowel sits between the M and L; non-native devs also tend to over-articulate the trailing -L.

coze.notes: Coze (Chinese 扣子, kòuzi, "button") is ByteDance's no-code AI agent and chatbot-building platform, launched in 2024 (open-source Coze Studio at github.com/coze-dev/coze-studio; coze.com). It is ONE syllable, KOHZ — identical to the rare/literary English verb "coze" ("to chat cozily"), rhyming with "doze" and "rose"; the trailing "e" is silent and there is no second vowel. Non-native devs routinely misread the -oze/-e ending as a two-syllable "CO-zee" (like "cozy"), which is wrong. It is non-obvious because the spelling looks like it should split, and the Chinese source name kòuzi actually does have two syllables. Pronunciation confirmed against Merriam-Webster (/ˈkōz/) and Dictionary.com (/koʊz/, respelling [kohz], one syllable); no official ByteDance pronunciation statement was found, hence community-consensus rather than creator-clarified.

cudnn.notes: cuDNN is NVIDIA's CUDA Deep Neural Network library, a GPU-accelerated set of primitives (convolution, attention, matmul, pooling, normalization) that underpins PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. Say it COO-dee-en-en: the lowercase 'cu' inherits CUDA's 'coo' sound (as in barra-CUDA), then D-N-N is spelled out letter by letter, with primary stress on COO. Non-native devs commonly misread the prefix as 'see-you' (C-U) or 'cud', which sounds wrong to practitioners. It's non-obvious because 'cu' is a CUDA abbreviation glued onto DNN, not a standalone English syllable.

dyad.notes: Dyad is a free, local, open-source AI app builder for power users (a v0 / Lovable / Bolt / Replit alternative), at dyad.sh (GitHub: dyad-sh/dyad). The name is the ordinary English/Greek word "dyad" (from Greek δυάς, "a pair/group of two"), stressed on the first syllable: DY-ad /ˈdaɪæd/, where "dy" is a long-i diphthong like "die." Confirmed by Merriam-Webster, Cambridge, Oxford, and Wiktionary. Non-native devs commonly misread the "dy-" as /diː/ and say "DEE-ad." Official site confirms the name but gives no pronunciation guidance; pronunciation is dictionary-backed.

einsum.notes: Clip of "Einstein summation" — the tensor-contraction primitive in NumPy/PyTorch/JAX (np.einsum, torch.einsum); NumPy docs confirm it "Evaluates the Einstein summation convention on the operands" (docs state the meaning, not the pronunciation). Stress on the first syllable: INE-sum, where "ein" rhymes with the start of Einstein /aɪn/ (like "wine"). Non-native devs commonly say "EEN-sum" (/iːn/) or "AYN-sum" (/eɪn/, as in "rein"); the German "ei" digraph is /aɪ/, not /iː/ or /eɪ/. Non-obvious because the clipped "ein-" hides its origin in the surname Einstein.

fugatto.notes: Fugatto = "Foundational Generative Audio Transformer Opus 1," NVIDIA's Nov-2024 any-to-any generative audio model (text/audio prompts to create or transform music, voice, and sound). Pronounced foo-GAH-toh, stress on the second syllable (GAH = long "ah" as in father), echoing the Italian musical term "fugato" (in the manner of a fugue) — NVIDIA doubled the t for the backronym (Italian speakers note the real word is one-t "fugato"). NVIDIA never published an explicit pronunciation, so this reading is inferred from the fugato etymon (community-consensus, not creator-clarified). Non-native devs commonly flatten it to FOO-gat-oh or few-GAT-oh, misplacing stress and using a short American "a."

gemm.notes: GEMM stands for GEneral Matrix Multiply, the BLAS Level-3 routine C = αAB + βC and the single most-invoked GPU kernel in deep learning ("fp8 gemm", "grouped gemm"). One syllable, said exactly like the jewel GEM — /dʒɛm/. The double M just comes from the BLAS function names (sgemm/dgemm/cgemm) and is NOT a geminated or hard sound. Non-native devs often mis-say it as spelled-out "G-E-M-M"; it looks like an initialism and the doubled M invites a wrong reading.

horovod.notes: Horovod is Uber's open-source distributed deep-learning training framework (TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch, MXNet) built on the ring-allreduce communication pattern. Uber's engineering blog confirms the name comes from a traditional Russian folk dance in which performers dance with linked arms in a circle — mirroring how the training processes pass gradients around a ring; the blog gives no pronunciation. First-syllable stress (HOR-uh-vahd) is corroborated by community pronunciation guides (e.g. HowToPronounce). Note the Russian original хоровод is actually final-stressed (kho-ro-VOD); English devs anglicize to first-syllable stress, and some mis-guess huh-ROH-vahd (second-syllable).

hume.notes: Hume AI is an empathic-voice research lab whose flagship product is EVI (Empathic Voice Interface, a speech-to-speech API through EVI 3 in 2025); it is named after the 18th-century Scottish philosopher David Hume and ships a "David Hume" EVI voice. One syllable, stressed HYOOM — a y-glide plus "oom", rhyming with "room" (/hjuːm/), with a silent trailing e. Non-native devs commonly mangle it into two syllables ("HYOO-mee", "HOO-may"); the single-syllable /hjuːm/ is non-obvious because the final e is silent and hu- is a y-glide, not "hoo".

inngest.notes: Inngest is a durable-execution / workflow-orchestration platform for running reliable step functions and orchestrating AI workflows on serverless, servers, or the edge. The name is a stylized spelling of the verb "ingest" (it ingests events), so it is said in-JEST — /ɪnˈdʒɛst/ — with a soft g (dʒ) and stress on the SECOND syllable, identical to "ingest." The extra n is purely cosmetic and does not change the sound. Devs seeing the doubled "nn" often say INN-guest or force a hard g, which is what makes it non-obvious. The ingest pronunciation is a well-supported community inference from the obvious wordplay; no official creator statement on pronunciation was found.

junie.notes: Junie is JetBrains' AI coding agent (launched April 2025; moved out of beta to general availability June 17, 2026), running inside JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, GoLand, Rider, CLion, PhpStorm, RubyMine) and as a standalone CLI. Said "JOO-nee" — stress on the first syllable, long "oo" — exactly like the English given name/nickname Junie (as in the children's-book character Junie B. Jones): "June" + "-ee". JetBrains has not published an explicit pronunciation, so this reflects the standard name pronunciation (community consensus), corroborated by HowToPronounce (dʒˈuː.ni) and PronounceNames. Non-native devs are misled by the French-looking "-ie" tail into "joo-NYAY", or read the u with the "fun" vowel as "JUN-ee"; both are wrong. Non-obvious because the spelling doesn't cue the long-"oo" vowel or the front-stressed name.

kiro.notes: Kiro is Amazon/AWS's agentic IDE (2025), centered on spec-driven development: it turns prompts into structured requirements, design, and tasks that AI agents implement. Stress on the first syllable — KEER-oh (/ˈkɪroʊ/). The official About page explicitly says the name "rhymes with hero." Non-native devs are pulled by the spelling toward "KY-roh" (long-i, like gyro or Cairo), which is wrong; the written i maps to a near-/ee/ vowel, not the long-i diphthong.

kokoro.notes: Kokoro is the viral 82M-parameter, Apache-2.0 open-weight TTS model released in 2025 by hexgrad; the name is the Japanese word 心, meaning 'heart'/'spirit'. The even, flat-vowel KOH-koh-roh — matching the original Japanese 心 (ko-ko-ro) — is how practitioners actually say it, and is used here as the primary. The creator's model-card quickstart phonemizes it /kˈOkəɹO/ (Misaki notation, capital O = /oʊ/) = KOH-kuh-roh, with a reduced middle vowel — kept as the cited alternate. Non-native devs commonly stress the middle and add an 'or' vowel ('ko-KOR-oh'), which is wrong; stress stays light and front. Model card verified 2026-07: 82M params, Apache 2.0, name meaning, and the /kˈOkəɹO/ phonemization all present.

le chat.notes: Le Chat is Mistral AI's consumer chatbot, a French pun since "chat" means both "chat" and "cat," which went viral in France in early 2025. Say it the French way, luh SHAH: the final t is silent and "ch" is /ʃ/ (like English "sh"). Wikipedia gives strict French /lə ʃa/; American speakers naturally render the vowel as "shah" /ʃɑ/. English devs commonly mangle it as "lee chat" or "luh CHAT" with a hard English "ch" and an audible t. Non-obvious because the spelling looks like the English word "chat," but it is French. (Mistral renamed the app Vibe at the end of May 2026.)

machete.notes: Neural Magic's mixed-input (w4a16/w8a16) GEMM kernel in vLLM 0.6.2+, optimized for NVIDIA Hopper GPUs; the spiritual successor to the Marlin kernels. Reuses the common English word 'machete' (the blade; a Spanish loanword). Standard General American puts stress on the second syllable — muh-SHET-ee — with 'ch' pronounced /ʃ/ ('sh') and the final -e as 'ee' /i/, not silent. Merriam-Webster and Wiktionary also list a 'ch' variant /məˈtʃɛti/ (muh-CHET-ee). Devs unfamiliar with the word commonly misread it as 'muh-CHEET' (rhyming with 'machine') or 'MATCH-et'. The source names and describes the kernel but gives no pronunciation guidance, so confidence is community-consensus (standard dictionary word), not creator-clarified.

marey.notes: Marey is Moonvalley's foundational AI video model (beta March 2025, GA July 2025), trained exclusively on licensed footage and marketed as the first "commercially safe" model for professional filmmaking; API available via fal.ai. Named after 19th-century French chronophotographer Étienne-Jules Marey. Fast Company and other press state it is pronounced exactly like the given name "Mary" — MAIR-ee, stress on the first syllable. The common mistake is a French-style "mah-RAY" (/maˈreɪ/) or spelling-based "MAR-ee," non-obvious because the honoree's French surname is [maʁɛ] (ma-REH) yet the product deliberately anglicizes to "Mary."

minio.notes: MinIO is a high-performance, S3-compatible object-storage server; the name is short for "minimal I/O," so it is said min-EYE-oh — the "IO" is the spelled-out input/output acronym ("eye-oh"). Founder/CEO A.B. (Anand Babu) Periasamy has explicitly stated he wants it known as "Min-IO, as in minimal IO" (TechTarget, verified). The article confirms the common variant: "most people pronounce it MIN-EO" (min-ee-oh, captured as the alt) — devs default to it because "-io" reads like an ordinary word ending rather than a spelled-out acronym.

orpheus.notes: Orpheus is Canopy Labs' 2025 open-source, Llama-3b-based TTS model (repo canopyai/Orpheus-TTS; HF canopylabs/orpheus-3b-*), named after the legendary Greek bard-musician. Stress on the first syllable: OR-fee-us. Non-native devs often shift stress (or-FEE-us) or read 'ph' as /p/ ('OR-pee-us'); 'ph' is /f/ and the accent stays up front. Non-obvious because the classical name is unfamiliar and 'eu' invites a false /juː/ reading — a rarer two-syllable variant OR-fyoos /ˈɔːrfjuːs/ does exist, but OR-fee-us is dominant.

phind.notes: Phind was an AI search engine and coding assistant built for developers (YC S22, founded by Michael Royzen and Justin Wei; the product went inactive in early 2026). The name is a play on "find," with "Ph" taking the F sound as in "phone" — a single stressed syllable, FIND (/faɪnd/), a homophone of the word "find." Non-native devs sometimes misread the unusual "Ph"-before-consonant spelling as "P-hind" or split it into two syllables; it is simply pronounced identically to "find." Corroborated by a Latent Space podcast where founder Michael Royzen's spoken "Phind" is transcribed as "Find." The pronunciation is universally understood but not explicitly stated in any official source, hence community-consensus.

qodo.notes: Qodo (formerly CodiumAI/Codium, rebranded 2024) is an AI code-review and test-generation agent platform. Its official rebrand blog confirms the name is a confluence of "Quality" and "Code" (and "quality of development") — but the blog does NOT state a pronunciation. Said KOH-doh: first syllable like "code," stress on syllable one. Pronunciation is community consensus (howtopronounce lists /ˈkoʊdoʊ/, "koh-doh"), consistent with the "Code" root — not an explicit creator statement. Non-native devs, thrown by a bare Q with no following U, often overcorrect to KWOH-doh (treating Q like "queen"/"quick"), but no /kw/ glide belongs here — this Q is a stylized C, not a normal English Q.

rocm.notes: ROCm is AMD's open-source GPU-compute software stack — the CUDA equivalent — and the runtime behind AI training/inference on Instinct accelerators (e.g. MI300X). Said "ROCK-em" (/ˈrɑkəm/): stress on the first syllable, trailing lowercase 'm' voiced as a light "em" (never spelled out); "rock-M" is effectively the same sound. Common mistakes: reading it letter-by-letter (R-O-C-M) or giving "rock" a wrong vowel ("Rockham"/"Rockcam"). Non-obvious because the lowercase 'm' disguises the origin "Radeon Open Compute (platform)"; per Wikipedia the name no longer functions as a true acronym (Open Compute is a registered trademark).

rodin.notes: Rodin (Hyper3D Rodin) is Deemos Technology's image-to-3D and text-to-3D generative model (2024-2025), named after French sculptor Auguste Rodin. Anglicized as roh-DAN: stress on the SECOND syllable, final '-DAN' rhyming with the name Dan (/dæn/), per Wikipedia's English IPA /roʊˈdæn/. Non-native devs wrongly read it as 'ROH-din' or 'ROD-in' with English spelling stress; it's non-obvious because the French-derived name stresses the final syllable and its nasal ending anglicizes to '-an' rather than '-in'.

sycl.notes: SYCL is a Khronos Group royalty-free C++ standard for heterogeneous/accelerator (GPU) computing, central to Intel oneAPI and CUDA-to-SYCL porting. Pronounced SICK-ul (/ˈsɪkəl/) — a deliberate homophone of the farming tool "sickle" — with stress on the first syllable. Non-native devs commonly spell it out "S-Y-C-L" or say "SY-cull" (/ˈsaɪkəl/), missing that the two-syllable name reads like a real English word. Originally "System-wide Compute Language"; since 2020 the creators state it is a name, not an acronym. Pronunciation documented on Wikipedia; no creator/official pronunciation quote located, so labeled community-consensus.

ulysses.notes: DeepSpeed-Ulysses is Microsoft's sequence-parallelism system for training extreme-long-context Transformers — it partitions input sequences across GPUs and uses an all-to-all collective for distributed attention (arXiv:2309.14509). "Ulysses" is the Latin name for Homer's Odysseus. Primary stress is on the second syllable: yoo-LISS-eez. Non-native devs commonly mangle it as "oo-LISS-es," missing that the initial U is /juː/ ("yoo") and the -es ending is /iːz/ ("eez"). A first-syllable-stress variant (YOO-lih-seez) exists but the second-syllable reading dominates. Non-obvious because it is a classical proper name with irregular English vowels, yet cited constantly in long-context training papers.

vapi.notes: Vapi (YC W21, vapi.ai) is a developer platform for building voice AI agents; co-founded by Jordan Dearsley (CEO) and Nikhil Gupta (CTO) — not a single founder. The name compresses "Voice API" (every feature is accessible via its API), confirmed by multiple sources. Stress falls on the FIRST syllable with a short /æ/: VAP-ee, rhyming with "happy" (/ˈvæpi/); corroborated by HowToPronounce (/vˈæ.pi/, "va-pee"). No official creator pronunciation statement was found, so confidence stays community-consensus. Non-native devs often default to "VAH-pee" (/ˈvɑpi/) or "VAY-pie" (/ˈveɪpaɪ/) because the bare letters V-A-P-I don't cue the short /æ/. For macOS say, respell as "vappy" for a reliable short-a.

vidu.notes: Vidu is the text-to-video AI model from Beijing Shengshu Technology and Tsinghua University (launched April 2024; Vidu Q1 shipped 2025). Stress falls on the FIRST syllable: VEE-doo. Shengshu chief scientist Zhu Jun explained at the 2024 Zhongguancun Forum that the name evokes "video" and the phrase "we do." Common mistake: non-native devs read it as VY-doo (long-i, as in "vie") or VID-oo (short-i, as in "video"); the correct branded vowel is a long /iː/, which is non-obvious because English "video" actually starts with a short-i /vɪd/.

sakana.notes: Sakana AI is the Tokyo research lab behind the AI Scientist and evolutionary model merging, co-founded by David Ha and Llion Jones; the name is the Japanese word for fish (魚 / さかな), evoking a school of fish forming a coherent whole from simple rules. Japanese says it flat and even-weighted — sah kah nah, three even morae (heiban, no accented syllable). English speakers commonly anglicize it to suh-KAH-nuh; the reading to avoid is the banana-rhyming suh-KAN-uh, which flattens the middle vowel to the wrong quality.

surya.notes: Surya is Datalab's open-source OCR, layout and table-recognition model and Python package (surya-ocr), the engine behind Marker. The README says it is named for the Hindu sun god, Sanskrit/Hindi sources support SOOR-yuh (two syllables, long oo, y-glide), while English Wikipedia gives the anglicized three-syllable SOO-ree-uh; both are attested and no source establishes a winner. The common mistake is reading the first vowel like surrey.

sarvam.notes: Sarvam AI is India's IndiaAI-Mission sovereign-model builder; its open Sarvam-M, Sarvam-30B and Sarvam-105B models ship on Hugging Face and are the default reference point for Indic-language LLM work. The name is Sanskrit सर्वम् "all, everything", where the inherent short a is a central /ə~ɐ/ — so it is SUR-vum, stressed on the first syllable, not "SAR-vam" with the vowel of "car".

kuaishou.notes: Kuaishou (快手, pinyin kuàishǒu, "quick hand") is the Chinese short-video company behind the Kling video model and the Kwai-Keye / KwaiVGI open models on Hugging Face. "Kuai" is one syllable that rhymes with "why" — which is exactly why the company's overseas brand is spelled Kwai — and "shou" rhymes with "show". So it is KWAI-show, not "koo-eye-shoo" or "KWAY-shoo".

jina.notes: Jina AI is the Berlin-based search-AI company (acquired by Elastic in October 2025) behind the jina-embeddings and jina-reranker model families and the r.jina.ai reader endpoint used in RAG and agent stacks. Its official FAQ answers "How do I pronounce Jina?" with: roughly like jee-na, stress on the jee — exactly the way the English name "Gina" is said. The same FAQ explicitly calls out jy-na and jee-NA (stress on the second syllable) as incorrect, so the Spanish-style HEE-nah and German-style YEE-nah readings the spelling suggests are wrong too.

vlt.notes: vlt is a JavaScript package manager and CLI from npm's creator Isaac Schlueter and former npm maintainers, released with vsr, a serverless registry. Because the name is written with no vowels, cold readers spell it out as "V-L-T" or guess "vult", and many write-ups call it "vault" — but the project brands itself "vlt /vōlt/" in its own GitHub org name and site titles, so it is one syllable, exactly like the English word volt, rhyming with bolt.

cogito.notes: Cogito is Deep Cogito's family of open-weight reasoning models (Cogito v2, and the v2.1 671B MoE on Hugging Face), named after Descartes' Latin cogito ergo sum. The stress lands on the first syllable in English — never "koh-JEE-toh" — but the g is unsettled: Merriam-Webster and Collins both lead with the hard-g KAH-gi-toh, while the anglicized soft-g KAH-ji-toh (by analogy with cogitate) and the Latinate KOH-ghee-toh are also attested. Deep Cogito has never stated a preference, and note this is a different word from the existing cognito entry (AWS Cognito, kog-NEE-toh).

typst.notes: Typst is a Rust-based markup typesetting system pitched as a modern LaTeX replacement, driven from the command line with typst compile. Its official README settles the pronunciation with IPA /taɪpst/: one single syllable, "Ty" as in Typesetting plus "pst" as in Hipster. It is NOT "typist" — there is no vowel between the p and the st.

qoder.notes: Alibaba's agentic AI IDE (Quest Mode, spec-driven agents), launched Aug 2025 with 1.0 in May 2026. Pronounced exactly like "coder" — the Q is a straight substitution for the C and adds no sound of its own. Chinese launch coverage describes the name as 谐音 Coder (a homophone of "Coder"); the common misreadings are the letter-name "Q-oder" and "KWOH-der".

grype.notes: Grype is Anchore's open-source vulnerability scanner for container images, filesystems and SBOMs, the companion to Syft. Anchore's official docs spell it out: "Grype (rhymes with hype)" — so it is simply the word "gripe". The y-spelling misleads readers into "grip", "GRIP-ee" or a French-looking "gree-PAY", all of which are wrong.

syft.notes: Anchore's open-source CLI that generates a Software Bill of Materials from container images and filesystems, and the SBOM feeder for its vulnerability scanner Grype. Anchore's own docs gloss it "pronounced like sift" — one syllable, exactly like the verb. The y trips people up because sibling tool Grype takes the other y sound and rhymes with hype, so readers guess "SYE-ft".

shai-hulud.notes: Shai-Hulud is the self-replicating npm supply-chain worm that hit thousands of packages from September 2025 through the "Shai-Hulud 2.0" and "Mini Shai-Hulud" waves, drawing a CISA alert and Microsoft/Unit 42 advisories; it is named for the Dune sandworm. Say it "shy-hoo-LOOD" with stress on the last syllable, from Arabic shay' khulud ("thing of immortality") — the reading used in the Dune films and in Frank Herbert's own recorded readings. Cold readers reach for "SHAY hyoo-lud" or "shy-HULL-ud"; a reduced middle vowel ("shy-huh-LOOD") is a valid variant.

ghidra.notes: Ghidra is the NSA's open-source software reverse-engineering framework, widely used for binary analysis and now a common target for AI agent MCP servers. The 'gh' spelling tempts readers into a soft-G 'JEE-dra', but the official FAQ specifies a hard G as in GitHub with stress on the first syllable: GEE-druh. Wikipedia gives the same IPA, and the question was settled in the project's own GitHub issue #61.

odysseus.alt_respelling_us: oh diss yoos|uh diss ee us
odysseus.notes: Odysseus is PewDiePie's MIT-licensed self-hosted AI workspace (chat, agents, deep research, local models via Ollama/llama.cpp/vLLM), which went from zero to roughly 30k GitHub stars in 48 hours and past 77k within three weeks. The name is the Greek hero of the Odyssey and the stress lands on the second syllable: oh-DISS-ee-us, not oh-dee-SAY-us or o-DIE-see-us. The compressed variant oh-DISS-yoos is also standard English, and an unstressed first syllable (uh-DISS-ee-us) is the form Wikipedia lists.

melange.notes: melange is Chainguard's declarative apk build tool; paired with apko it builds Wolfi-based low-CVE container images without a Dockerfile. The name is the French word mélange ("mixture"), so the final -ange is a soft "zh" like rouge or measure, and the stress falls on the second syllable: may-LAHNZH, not "MEL-anj". The anglicized may-LAHNJ is a listed dictionary variant, but the zh-form is the standard one.

spiffe.notes: SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework For Everyone) is a CNCF graduated standard that gives every workload a cryptographic identity (an SVID) so services can do zero-trust mTLS without baked-in secrets; its implementation is SPIRE. It is spoken as a word, not letter-by-letter, and the trailing -E is voiced: it is pronounced exactly like the English word "spiffy", rhyming with jiffy. The two traps are saying "spiff" with a silent E, or spelling it out S-P-I-F-F-E.

youki.notes: Container runtime implementing the OCI runtime-spec in Rust, a runc alternative. The official README and docs state it is pronounced /joʊki/ or "yoh-key", after the Japanese word 容器 (yōki) meaning "a container". The "ou" is a Japanese long o, so it is not "YOO-kee".

krkn.notes: Krkn is a CNCF sandbox chaos and resiliency testing tool for Kubernetes, originally built at Red Hat as "Kraken". The vowel-less spelling is pure branding: it is simply said "kraken", which the maintainers confirm in the README title "Krkn aka Kraken" and in the code (the entry point is still run_kraken.py). Dictionaries give KRAH-ken as the standard reading of kraken, with KRAY-ken and KRACK-en as common variants.

aeraki.notes: Aeraki Mesh is a CNCF sandbox project that manages non-HTTP layer-7 protocols such as Dubbo, Thrift and Redis in an Istio service mesh. The maintainers spell the pronunciation out in the README: Aeraki [Air-rah-ki] is the Greek word for breeze, with the stress on the second syllable. The ae- digraph misleads English readers into air-uh-kye or ay-RAY-kee.

kured.notes: Kured (KUbernetes REboot Daemon) is a CNCF Sandbox DaemonSet that performs safe automatic node reboots, widely used on AKS and self-managed clusters. Its official FAQ answers the pronunciation question directly: say it as "cure dee", because the trailing D comes from "daemon", akin to systemd. The obvious cold reading — one syllable rhyming with "cured" — is the common mistake; the D is spoken separately, exactly as in containerd and etcd.

fluss.notes: Apache Fluss (incubating) is a Flink-native streaming storage layer for real-time lakehouses, tiering data into Iceberg or Paimon. The name is the German word for "river" and the project README states it is pronounced /flus/ — a short German u, closest to English "floos" — so it is not English "fuss", and the double-s stays voiceless rather than turning into a z.

zenoh.notes: Eclipse Zenoh is a zero-overhead pub/sub, store/query and compute protocol, now an official ROS 2 middleware via rmw_zenoh. Its README annotates it "(pronounced /zeno/)" — the trailing h is silent and it is just two syllables, never "ZEE-noh-h" or "zeh-NOH". English speakers usually say it like the philosopher Zeno, while the maintainers' own /zeno/ notation and many European users give ZEH-noh.

kuasar.notes: Kuasar is a CNCF Sandbox multi-sandbox container runtime written in Rust (Huawei Cloud, WasmEdge, openEuler) that unifies MicroVM, Wasm and app-kernel sandboxes behind one sandbox API. The name is simply "quasar" respelled with a K for Kubernetes — the maintainers' own CNCF application glosses it as "Kuasar(Quasar in Kubernetes)" — so it is said KWAY-zar, exactly like the astronomical object. Cold readers routinely mangle it as "koo-AH-sar" by reading the ua literally.

kairos.notes: Kairos is a CNCF Sandbox project: an immutable Linux meta-distribution for building bootable edge Kubernetes OS images, used alongside Talos and Flatcar in edge fleets. The name is Ancient Greek καιρός, "the opportune moment", and the 'ai' digraph is /aɪ/ with stress on the first syllable: KYE-ross. Readers coming to it cold often say "KAY-ross" or "kah-ee-ROSS", both of which are wrong; a valid variant ends in -rohs.

piraeus.notes: Piraeus Datastore is a CNCF sandbox project providing LINSTOR/DRBD-based replicated block storage for Kubernetes, a common Longhorn/OpenEBS alternative on bare-metal clusters; it is named after Greece's great port, Πειραιάς. The dominant English reading is "py-REE-us" — the -aeus ending is a trap that invites "PIE-ray-oos" or stress on the first syllable. Note that "pih-RAY-us" is not an error but a documented secondary variant: Wikipedia, Collins and Dictionary.com all list both /paɪˈriːəs/ and /pɪˈreɪəs/.

vineyard.notes: Vineyard (v6d) is a CNCF sandbox project — an in-memory immutable data manager giving zero-copy sharing of distributed data between steps of big-data and ML pipelines. Despite the spelling it is VIN-yerd, with a short i and a silent e; it is not VINE-yard. Dictionaries are unanimous on /ˈvɪnjərd/, and the project site offers no alternative pronunciation of its own.

gravitino.notes: Apache Gravitino is a federated metadata lake / open data catalog (originally from Datastrato) that competes with Unity Catalog and Polaris for Iceberg governance. The name is the physics particle — graviton plus the Italian diminutive -ino — and dictionaries give grav-i-TEE-noh, stress on 'tee', with 'vi' as a short 'ih' rather than 'vye'. The project publishes no pronunciation guidance of its own, so the dictionary reading of the particle is the consensus.

jooq.notes: jOOQ is a typesafe SQL DSL for Java that models SQL as an internal DSL, widely used as an alternative to JPA/Hibernate. It is a recursive acronym for "jOOQ Object Oriented Querying", but it is never spelled out letter-by-letter — the maintainers keep a dedicated page stating it is pronounced /dʒuːk/, exactly like the "juke" in jukebox. The official blog compares it to SQL: just as you say "sequel" rather than "ess queue ell", you say "juke" rather than "jay o o queue".

rocq.notes: The Rocq Prover is the new name of the Coq proof assistant, official since the 9.0 release in March 2025. It is pronounced simply "rock" — the name honors Inria Rocquencourt, where the system was born, and echoes the mythical bird Roc; the final q is silent. Do not carry over the old Coq /kɒk/ vowel or read the French -cq as "kew".

ktor.notes: Ktor is JetBrains' Kotlin-first framework for building asynchronous servers and clients, and the default HTTP stack for Kotlin backends and Kotlin Multiplatform. The official FAQ says it is pronounced /keɪ-tor/ — the name is the abbreviation ctor (constructor) with the C replaced by K for Kotlin, so the leading K is said as the letter name. Readers who see the vowel-less Kt spelling often guess kuh-TOR or spell it out K-T-O-R; both are wrong.

hikari.notes: Hikari is the Japanese word 光 ("light; ray") and the name behind HikariCP, the high-performance JDBC connection pool that Spring Boot uses by default — hence the ubiquitous "HikariPool-1 - Starting..." log line. The README's own heading spells the pronunciation out as Hi·ka·ri [hi·ka·'lē]: hee-kah-REE, three even syllables with the Japanese flapped r sounding close to an English l. English readers commonly get it wrong as "HIGH-kah-ree" with a long English i; the trailing CP (Connection Pool) is then spelled out as C-P.

werkzeug.notes: The WSGI library under Flask, German for "tool" — Pallets' own project page says so. German spelling trips up English readers four ways: W is a V, z is "ts", eu is "oy", and the final g devoices to a k, giving "VERK-tsoyk". The common English readings "WERK-zoog" and "WORK-zeeg" are simply wrong.

jinja.notes: Jinja is the Python template engine behind Flask, Ansible and dbt, and the format Hugging Face chat templates are written in. The official FAQ says the name is the Japanese word 神社 (jinja, a Shinto shrine), chosen because temple puns on template — so both j's are soft: JIN-juh. Non-native readers who give j its native value and say "YIN-ya" or "HIN-ha" are the common miss.

zephyr.notes: Zephyr is the Linux Foundation's small-footprint RTOS for microcontrollers, and the default runtime behind Nordic's nRF Connect SDK plus growing Espressif and ST support. It is pronounced ZEF-ur (/ˈzɛfər/) — stress on the first syllable, a short 'e', the 'ph' as /f/, and the final 'y' reduced to a schwa — after Zephyrus, the Greek god of the west wind. Non-native devs routinely misread the Greek-via-Latin spelling as ZEE-fire, zeh-FEER or ZEP-hire.

zeitwerk.notes: Zeitwerk is the Ruby code loader that has been Rails' default autoloader since Rails 6 and remains so in Rails 8 The name is German for "clockwork" and follows German spelling rules — Z is "ts", "ei" is "eye", W is "v" — giving TSITE-verk, not the common misreading "ZEET-werk".

citus.notes: Citus is the open-source extension that turns PostgreSQL into a distributed, sharded database, and it powers Azure Cosmos DB for PostgreSQL. It is pronounced SITE-us, not KEE-tus or SIT-us — the name comes from the Olympic motto Citius, Altius, Fortius (faster, higher, stronger). Citus Data got asked so often that they published an official blog post settling it.

apisix.notes: Apache APISIX is a top-level Apache Software Foundation cloud-native API gateway and AI gateway, a peer of Kong, Traefik and Envoy. Despite looking like one word, it is spoken as two tokens: the letters A-P-I followed by the number six. Maintainer @membphis confirmed this on the project tracker, explaining that 6 is a lucky number in China — hence the name.

alembic.notes: Alembic is the schema-migration tool for SQLAlchemy, used in most FastAPI and SQLModel backends. The name comes from the alchemical still, borrowed from Arabic al-anbiq via Greek ambix. Stress falls on the middle syllable — uh-LEM-bik — not on the first, which is what the spelling tempts most non-native speakers into.

reqwest.notes: reqwest is the de-facto HTTP client crate of the Rust ecosystem, written by the author of hyper. The name is a deliberate misspelling of "request": the creator's introduction post explains it was forced on him because the `request` crate name was already taken, and it is said exactly like the ordinary English word "request". Cold readers often guess "REK-west" because the qw digraph is almost nonexistent in English outside "qwerty".

cython.notes: Optimizing compiler that turns Python-like .pyx code into C extension modules; it powers the fast paths in NumPy, SciPy, pandas and scikit-learn. Pronounced "SIGH-thon" — the C is soft, rhyming with Python, not "see-thon" or "kye-thon". Do not confuse it with CPython, the reference interpreter, which really is said "see-Python".

leiningen.notes: Leiningen (the lein command) is the long-standing build tool and project-automation entry point for Clojure. The official FAQ settles it: LINE-ing-en, with the German ei read as "eye" — the name comes from Carl Stephenson's short story "Leiningen Versus the Ants". Common misreadings like "lay-nin-jen" or "lee-nin-gen" are simply wrong.

rngd.notes: RNGD is FuriosaAI's second-generation data-center inference NPU (48GB HBM3, 180W, tensor-contraction architecture), pitched as a power-efficient Nvidia alternative and in mass production since January 2026. The vowel-less name is a disemvoweled spelling of "renegade" — Furiosa's own product page and its Hot Chips 2024 press release both gloss it as RNGD (pronounced "renegade"), and the company brands its spec page and user summit as RENEGADE. Reading it letter-by-letter as R-N-G-D or guessing "ringed" is the common mistake.

tau-bench.notes: Sierra's τ-bench (now τ²/τ³-bench) is the tool-agent-user benchmark whose pass^k scores are cited in most 2025-2026 model cards. The leading character is the Greek letter tau, and the maintainers' README answers "How do you say τ³-bench?" with "We just say 'tau three'" — so it is the letter name, never "TAO" as in Daoism. In General American tau is /taʊ/, rhyming with "now"; the /tɔː/ ("taw") variant is also widely heard.

agntcy.notes: AGNTCY is the Linux Foundation-hosted "Internet of Agents" project donated by Cisco (Outshift), covering agent discovery (OASF), messaging (SLIM) and identity, and is routinely compared against MCP and A2A. It is a disemvoweled brand — AGNT (agent) plus -CY — and is simply read as the English word "agency" (AY-jun-see), not spelled out letter-by-letter. Cold readers usually try "A-G-N-T-C-Y" or "ag-EN-tik-ee"; the project's own site and the Linux Foundation press release never state the pronunciation, so this is labeled community-consensus on the strength of third-party technical coverage.

gaia.notes: GAIA (General AI Assistants) is the 466-question benchmark for browsing and tool-use agents, with a live Hugging Face leaderboard and its own unit in the HF Agents Course. The name reuses the Greek earth goddess, which English reads two ways: dictionaries list both /ˈɡaɪə/ (guy-uh) and /ˈɡeɪə/ (gay-uh). The authors never stated an official pronunciation, but scientific naming after the goddess — Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis — is settled on guy-uh, so that is the safer default.

sycophancy.notes: In LLM work, sycophancy is the failure mode where a model excessively agrees with or flatters the user, and it is now a standard eval axis reported in system cards and release notes. Stress falls on the FIRST syllable — SICK-uh-fuhn-see — and the opening "sy" is /sɪ/ like "sick", not "sigh". The tempting si-KOF-uhn-see reading, formed by analogy with discrepancy or despondency, is not recorded in any major dictionary; note also that the adjective sycophantic does shift the stress, to sik-uh-FAN-tik.

elo.notes: The rating scale behind LMArena / Chatbot Arena leaderboards, cited as "Elo score" in most 2025-2026 model launch posts and eval blogs. It is the surname of Hungarian-American physics professor Arpad Elo (né Élő), not an acronym — Wikipedia states it is "written as 'Elo', not 'ELO'" — so spelling it out as letters E-L-O is the common mistake. Both EE-loh and EL-oh are accepted in English, with EE-loh listed first by Wikipedia and Wiktionary.

liger.notes: LinkedIn's Liger-Kernel is a Triton kernel library for LLM training, giving roughly 20% more throughput and 60% less memory, and it is wired into TRL, Axolotl, LLaMA-Factory and the Hugging Face Trainer. The name is the lion-tiger hybrid, so it is "LY-ger" with a long i and a hard g — not "LEE-ger", not short-i "LIG-er", and not soft-g "LY-jer". LinkedIn also backronyms it as GPU Efficient Runtime, but it is always spoken as the animal word.

helion.notes: Helion is PyTorch's high-level kernel-authoring DSL from Meta (public beta at PyTorch Conference 2025), which compiles down to Triton. The name is the physics term for a helium-3 nucleus, a deliberate sibling to Triton (the hydrogen-3 nucleus), and it is a portmanteau of helium + ion — so the first syllable is HEE as in helium, not HELL as in the English word hellion. The reduced form hee lee uhn is an equally valid variant.

chutes.notes: Chutes is Rayon Labs' serverless AI inference platform on Bittensor Subnet 64, and one of the highest-throughput providers routable from OpenRouter. The name is the ordinary English word chute (as in parachute or a garbage chute), borrowed from French, so the ch is /ʃ/ and it sounds exactly like shoots. Non-native developers reading it cold in a provider list routinely say chootz with an English ch, which is wrong.

ion stoica.notes: Romanian-American Berkeley professor: co-creator of Apache Spark, Mesos and Ray, co-founder of Databricks and Anyscale, and head of the Sky Computing Lab behind vLLM and Chatbot Arena. "Ion" is not the chemistry word — it is the Romanian form of "John", /iˈon/, so "ee-OHN" and not "EYE-on". The surname is "STOY-kuh": the "oi" is a single diphthong (the name goes back to Bulgarian Stoyko), not "sto-EE-ka" or "STOH-ik-uh".

ghodsi.notes: Ali Ghodsi is the co-founder and CEO of Databricks, quoted in most lakehouse and AI-infrastructure news cycles. The surname is Persian (قدسی, from Ghods/Quds, "sacred"), where the "gh" transliterates a single hard G rather than an aspirated or two-step "guh-HOD". It is simply "GOD-see", two syllables with the stress up front.

liang wenfeng.notes: Founder and CEO of DeepSeek and co-founder of the High-Flyer hedge fund; the R1/V3 line reset open-weight model economics. Pinyin is Liáng Wénfēng: "Liang" is a single syllable, lyahng — not "lee-ANG" — and "Wenfeng" is wun-fung, with Wiktionary giving /li̯ɑŋ³⁵/, /wən³⁵/ and /fɤŋ⁵⁵/ for 梁文锋. The usual Western podcast reading "lee-ANG wen-FENG", with an English short-e in both halves, is simply wrong.

raschka.notes: Sebastian Raschka, author of "Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)" and the Ahead of AI newsletter. German surname: "sch" is the /ʃ/ digraph, so it is "RAHSH-kuh", not a "rask" cluster. US speakers commonly anglicize the first vowel to "RASH-kuh"; both are heard.

tri dao.notes: Tri Dao is the author of FlashAttention and co-author of Mamba, and co-founder/Chief Scientist at Together AI; his name fronts the Dao-AILab kernels and Mamba model cards. The name is Vietnamese: "Tri" is TREE, not "try", and "Dao" (Đào) rhymes with "how" — it is neither the Taoist "tao" nor the crypto "DAO". VOA's Vietnamese pronunciation guide renders the surname Dao as "DOW" and the Tri- syllable as "TREE-".

ronacher.notes: Armin Ronacher is the Austrian developer behind Flask and Jinja, now at Sentry, whose essays on agentic coding are HN front-page staples. The surname is German: the 'ch' follows a back vowel, so it is the ach-Laut (the throat sound in Scottish 'loch'), which English speakers render as a /k/ — ROH-nah-ker with stress on the first syllable, never the English 'ch' of 'church' and never a 'sh'. Anglophones routinely mangle it (the top user entry on howtopronounce.com is 'rah-nashur'), and on Rustacean Station the host had to stop and ask him how to say it.

kolmogorov.notes: Andrey Kolmogorov, Russian mathematician behind Kolmogorov complexity and the theorem underpinning Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs). Stress lands on the third syllable — kohl-muh-GOR-uhf — and the final v is devoiced to an f, matching the Russian [kəlmɐˈɡorəf]. Cold readers usually get it wrong twice, stressing the first syllable and ending on a voiced ov.

huyen.notes: Chip Huyen, author of O'Reilly's 'AI Engineering' and 'Designing Machine Learning Systems' (huyenchip.com). Vietnamese Huyền is a single syllable — Wiktionary gives Hanoi [hwiən˨˩], roughly 'hw' plus the name Ian; English readers wrongly stretch it into three syllables as 'HYOO-yen'. Commonly simplified in English to rhyme with 'when'.

rubin.notes: "ROO-bin". NVIDIA GPU generation named after dark-matter astronomer Vera Rubin; paired with the Vera CPU, successor to Blackwell.

ami.notes: Two names share the letters: AWS’s Amazon Machine Image is read letter-by-letter A-M-I (with "ah-mee" a common informal reading), while Yann LeCun’s AMI Labs is creator-clarified as "ah-MEE" — French for "friend".

taalas.notes: Name derives from Hindi taalaa/taalasaaz ('lock'/'locksmith'), hence stressed long ah vowels, not 'TAY-las'.

jalapeño.notes: OpenAI/Broadcom inference processor; Spanish loanword with j = /h/ and ñ = /nj/.

omlx.notes: MLX-based Apple Silicon LLM inference server; said as "oh" plus the letters M-L-X, like its parent framework.

maia.notes: Microsoft Azure AI accelerator named for the Greek Pleiad; trade press ("My, oh Maia") leans MY-ah while the classical English reading is MAY-uh.

majorana.notes: Microsoft's topological quantum chip line named after physicist Ettore Majorana; English usage is my-uh-RAH-nuh — the Italian j is a glide, never /dʒ/.

mai.notes: Microsoft AI's in-house model family (MAI-Thinking-1, MAI-Voice-2, ...); read letter-by-letter like the "AI" it contains — Microsoft has published no official pronunciation.

corsair.notes: d-Matrix's in-memory-compute inference accelerator (also the PC-hardware brand); American dictionaries stress the first syllable — KOR-sair, not the French-style cor-SAIR.

lighttpd.notes: Portmanteau of light + httpd; the official site states it is pronounced "lighty".

immich.notes: Self-hosted photo/video backup server; creator Alex Tran: "It is pronounced as 'image'", though much of the community says IMM-itch.

debezium.notes: Official FAQ: "dee-BEE-zee-uhm" — say "DBs-ium" fast; from DBs + the periodic-table -ium suffix.

nifi.notes: Apache dataflow tool from NSA's "NiagaraFiles"; the official FAQ states "nye fye" is the preferred pronunciation.

keptn.notes: CNCF application life-cycle orchestration project (archived Sept 2025); official glossary states "Keptn, pronounced captain."

tyk.notes: Open-source Go API gateway; creator Martin Buhr confirmed on GitHub it rhymes with "Mike" — /taɪk/.

xapian.notes: FAQ: usually "ZAP-ian" like the word zap; "ZAY-pian" is a popular variant (name mangles "sapient").

kueue.notes: Kubernetes-native job-queueing controller (kubernetes-sigs); the K-wordplay name is read exactly like "queue" — one syllable, kyoo.

linode.notes: Cloud host (acquired by Akamai in 2022; now Akamai Connected Cloud), from "Linux node"; the official @linode account clarified "We pronounce it Lih-node - 'lin' as in Linux!", though LIE-node remains a common mispronunciation.

openbao.notes: Linux Foundation fork of HashiCorp Vault named after baozi, the steamed bun (per GitHub discussion #34) — "bao" rhymes with "cow", not "bay-oh".

sqitch.notes: Wheeler's database change-management tool ("SQL changes with Git stuck in the middle"); pronounced "skitch", not "skwitch".

guice.notes: Google's Java dependency-injection framework; the README opens with "Guice (pronounced 'juice')".

thymeleaf.notes: Java/Spring server-side template engine; the official FAQ spells it out as /ˈtaɪmˌlɪːf/ — th sounds like plain t, as in the herb thyme (TIME-leaf).

vitess.notes: CNCF-graduated MySQL sharding system from YouTube; named after French "vitesse" (speed), so stress falls on the second syllable.

musl.notes: Lightweight libc behind Alpine Linux images; official site says "musl, pronounced like the word 'mussel' or 'muscle'".

axios.notes: Promise-based HTTP client among npm's most-downloaded packages; from Greek axios ("worthy") with no official ruling — top-voted community reading is "AK-see-ohs".

mockito.notes: Java mocking framework whose name is a maintainer-confirmed pun on mock + mojito; a maintainer answered issue #889 with mock-based /mɑˈkiːtoʊ/ ("mah-KEE-toh"), while creator Szczepan Faber told the mailing list to "go ahead and pronounce it the mojito way" ("moh-KEE-toh"), so the split is genuine.

xonsh.notes: Python-powered cross-platform shell; the official homepage states it "sounds like 'consh'".

pyodide.notes: CPython compiled to WebAssembly for running Python in the browser; maintainers rth and hoodmane gave the IPA ˈpɑɪ.ə.ˌdɑɪd (Py + Iodide — rhymes with iodide).

haxe.notes: Cross-platform language compiling to JS, C++, JVM and more; early official docs said "hex" while English speakers naturally say "hacks" — no creator ruling, both survive at conferences.

fltk.notes: Veteran cross-platform C++ GUI toolkit; the official docs open with 'The Fast Light Tool Kit ("FLTK", pronounced "fulltick")'.

cygwin.notes: POSIX compatibility layer for Windows (Cygnus + Windows); Wikipedia gives /ˈsɪɡwɪn/ SIG-win, confirmed by a Cygwin developer on the project mailing list.

nagios.notes: Veteran open-source infrastructure monitoring system (NetSaint 1999, renamed 2002); creator Ethan Galstad's official FAQ spells it out as "nah-ghee-ose" — "Nah" plus the start of "geese" plus the end of "verbose" — so the g is hard, never "NAY-jee-os", and Wikipedia records the same /ˈnɑːɡiːoʊs/.
